sentences.text: Oh yes.
Do you know, the people who were left in the barracks, they did not have proper clothes?
And the weather started getting cold already.
You'd be surprised.
Even the late summer, it was cold.
And they didn't have proper clothes and proper shoes.
So when we were coming back from our work, we used to put on three, four, five dresses, one on top of the other.
And then we were-- smuggled in some lipsticks because the red stripe had to be on our back, no matter what we wore.
And then we would give it to the girls who didn't have.
We would take all our clothes and take their, you know, rag, go back next morning with that one rag.
And during the night, we put the red stripe on it with the lipstick.
And then the next day or two days later-- we couldn't do it every day.
We were afraid.
But that's how we used to smuggle in.
Sometimes we smuggled dresses, sometimes shoes, socks, whatever we were able.
And many times-- most of the time, we used to smuggle food.
Because they did not really have-- they had this horrible, horrible food, which was made-- it was a watery soup, which was made of, like, turnips.
But in Europe, they used this sort of a vegetable to feed the animals.
And this was in the soup, which was almost unbelievably bad.
And you would just-- had to get used to eating it and say, the only way I will survive-- if I eat the camp food.
So [SIGHS] we used to bring a little stuff here and there, a extra piece of bread, or if we-- whatever we found.
Not really, not by the women.
I heard by the men there was from my husband because he was also in the camp.
Not by the women.
First of all, we couldn't even think of that.
We were glad if we got a little something.
We were so hungry.
And everybody ate whatever.
Those who didn't eat did not survive.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because there was time-- all right, while I was working in [INAUDIBLE], I had what to eat.
But later on, when I'll come to that, there was nothing to eat.
And if you didn't eat that miserable, dirty water with a little turnip in it, you couldn't survive.
Oh yes, all the time.
SS, SS.
Matter of fact, once I was beaten up by one terribly because he caught me-- I was trying to take some extra clothes into the camp.
And he saw it, got me, and beat me up that I don't know how I got up.
But a couple of hours later, the girls told me that I, I came to myself.
Oh, yes.
They had such rubber-tip-- think, how do you call that-- whips.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Sure.
And that was right in their shoes, their boots, you know.
They all wore high boots.
And it was sticking to their high boots.
And that's what they used, those whips [LAUGHS] to whip them-- to whip us-- and many times, just their bare hands.
I haven't met them, not in Birkenau.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know.
I tried to get out of their way not to find out.
I never wanted to get under their hands.
The only time I was in, in contact with them-- when they beat me up.
So I [LAUGHS] can't tell you that they were a little better.
Well, that was a separate barrack, which was all toilets and showers.
And you had to go there.
It, it was a separate barrack in the middle of the camp.
More or less.
Except at night, they didn't let us.
But more or less, yes.
That, you were able to do.
Not really.
[SIGHS] We knew there was a war.
We heard some planes come.
And then we heard some rumors that there were bombs coming, and they were bombing certain factories, certain places.
But we really didn't know what was going on.
No.
No, that was later.
The only time we were aware was when we saw them already, [LAUGHS] when they came to liberate our camp-- no, no.
No, never.
I never saw them, not in Auschwitz-Birkenau.
We were away from the world.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We didn't believe the world existed, actually.
Because we had our own world, where we lived like animals.
I mean, go ask a dog if he knows there is a war going on in Bosnia.
This is how we were.
We didn't have newspapers, radios.
We were not human.
We were treated like animals.
The will to live and because we were young.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Also, a lot of miracles, like when Mengele lifted my right arm instead of the left.
I consider that a miracle.
Well, we knew them.
And one of the boy's sister was working with me.
And I knew him from my hometown.
And I knew him, that he was one of the Sonderkommando.
He was a young man, 16 years old.
And he was one of them.
And matter of fact, he was one of them who planned to blow up the-- one of the crematoriums.
I think about 15 boys or, I don't know, around that planned to blow up one of the crematoriums.
And we knew about it.
Because when we went to work, many times, they also went to work.
Even though they were coming from the man's camps, we were quite near each other.
And there-- we were getting signals.
Oh, a little piece of papers was thrown over.
So they told us about it.
And we knew.
And we knew when it would have to happened.
And they had a plan.
And unfortunately, the day they were supposed to blow up the-- one of the crematoriums-- we knew it would happen early in the morning.
All of a sudden, we hear the SS running all over, yelling, [GERMAN], which meant, lock your doors!
Everybody inside!
[SPEAKING GERMAN].
And then we knew what it was.
That was the sign that they caught them.
And that same day, they were all shot.
They were shot.
We never saw her brother again.
And some of the boys we never saw again.
Terrible.
Terrible.
Then we knew there is absolutely no escape, no escape.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was a tragedy.
No, never.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I never saw children.
I never saw old people, even older people.
Yes.
It There terrible.
[SIGHS] As we walked to our work, many times we saw long, long long lines of people coming towards us.
It seems by that time, they didn't even put them on the trucks, you know, on these open trucks to bring them.
They had to walk.
And they were never carrying their little whatever.
Yes, we saw them.
A very sad, interesting, and funny episode happened.
As these transfers were coming and we were taking away their clothes and their belongings, I found a beautiful evening bag, an exceptionally nice one.
And I went to pick it up.
And it was very heavy.
And when I opened it, I saw what was inside.
Somebody relieved themselves in that beautiful bag.
Even when they were going to their deaths, and no matter how they were treated, they still had the decency to use-- most probably that was her most precious thing-- to use this for that purpose.
It was so sad.
But somehow-- we looked at each other, with the girl next to me.
And we started to laugh.
You-- we laughed about sorrows.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was nothing to say.
I-- the reason I mention it-- because you should know there were still [NON-ENGLISH].
They were-- no matter how they wanted us to be animals, they still were decent human beings who tried to be decent.
It's a sad story.
But you have to laugh.
No, no, not really, no.
The boys would tell us when it was Yom Kippur, perhaps.
But life went on the same way.
There's nothing we could do about it.
We were just numb.
And most of the people said, well, what's the difference?
Where is God?
What are we going to do?
I mean, didn't pay any attention, most of us-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] most of us.
When did you realize that you might be leaving Auschwitz?
Oh, I didn't know it, not until the moment it happened, not until the moment it happened.
Once the transports were finished, there were no transports coming anymore.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The last transport was coming, if I remember good, from Lodz, from the Lodz Ghetto, which was towards the fall.
And then the transports stopped.
So our work was finished.
Then I was transferred to a place called the Weberei, where we used to do these [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] things.
They brought us some leather strips.
We used to make ribbons out of them.
And all sorts of things we were working there.
Maybe that was about the last month or so, the last six weeks.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The very last few weeks in Auschwitz-Birkenau was no more work, no more food.
It was nothing.
We were just laying in our barracks doing nothing, getting almost no food, and sitting and waiting.
Were a few selections.
And we were always less and less because some of them went to the-- to the crematorium.
We're left.
Until New Year's Eve, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a couple of SS came in to the barracks where we were and called out my number, 6684.
I was with a few girls together.
And they begged me not to go because I was the only one to call.
You know, you stick together.
A few girls-- in your sorrows, you stick together.
And I was the only one called then.
I told the girls, I believe in destiny.
Whatever has to happen to me-- if they'll take me to the gas chamber, that's where I'm going.
They called my number.
I'm going.
And I left them there.

That was the day-- the night when they took us, undressed us, took everything away from us, and they gave us one piece of so-called dress-- a summer dress, the 1st of January-- and put us into wagons.
We didn't know where we were going.
We didn't know what'll happen.
They put us in the wagons like sardines.
And in the middle of the wagon, there was a, a round space where there were four soldiers with a stove in the middle and blankets all around.
And then we realized they were not SS soldiers, but they were Wehmacht.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this is how I left Auschwitz.
I didn't know where we were going.
Matter of fact, I was very sick.
I was-- I had laryngitis.
And I couldn't talk.
But we were pushed to the two sides.
And this is how we left Auschwitz.
Yes, it was like cattle wagons, cattle trains, which had a cover.
But cattle trains-- very cold.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And one of the soldiers must have felt sorry for me-- would give me a couple of drinks that I felt better.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it took us-- I really don't even know how many days, maybe also four or five days, until we arrived to Bergen-Belsen.
Very little, just a piece of bread, you know, the same bread portions, and a little bit of drinks, nothing else.
Oh yes.
We had to put them out.
They fainted.
They got ill.
If they couldn't live-- and some of them died-- we just had to put them out.
Once a day, the train stopped.
They let us out to relieve ourselves.
Obviously, they didn't want to have any smell or dirt in the [LAUGHS] train where the soldiers had to be.
And then we had to get rid of those bodies.
It happened, quite a few.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And this is how [SIGHS] I left Auschwitz-Birkenau and arrived to Bergen-Belsen, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] another chapter in my life.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was beginning of January, obviously.
Well, we got out of the wagons-- when they let us out, they took us in to barracks also.
But there were no beds, nothing at all, only straw on the floor.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This Wehrmacht soldier hand me his blanket when I left.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He must have been 1% better than the others.
So I was very fortunate to have a blanket because nobody had a blanket.
We was lying in-- on the straw, nothing on it.
And under this blanket, we had, maybe, 10 girls, or as many as we could get under.
And we didn't know what's going to happen.
Food we didn't get.
We had absolutely no food the first few days.
People were starving and freezing.
We would go outside and try to find a little grass or, or something.
But, of course, in January, it's very hard to find-- not even grass you could.
And every day, the SS would come in every morning and pick some of the girls out to work.
They were making beds, you know, put pieces of board together and all sorts-- and clean up.
There was nobody in the camp.
We were the first ones.
Later, we found out that they took the transport out.
And of course, we were so weak and hungry and cold that really, we didn't want to work.
So every morning early, we got up.
And we ran to the bathrooms.
You know, that barrack, that long barrack which was called the bathroom, that's where we ran to hide.
That was the only place they really didn't come in after us.
But whomever they got before or after or on the run, they caught them and got them together to go to work.
I was fairly lucky.
And I avoided them for the-- quite a few days.
And it was till about 11 o'clock in the morning or whatever-- we had no watches-- but quite a few hours later that they took the people whom they wanted.
It quieted down.
So [? it was ?] from the bathroom, we ran back to the barrack and got under this cover, a few girls.
And we were happy to be there.
They left us alone.
And we tried to sleep, or-- we couldn't do much.
Until one day, [SIGHS] a few hours later, an SS came in and said to us-- I have to say it in German because it sounds different.
[SPEAKING GERMAN]!
He says, you pigs!
Out of here!
We had no place-- so we had to get up.
There was no other people, just us.
We were about six or seven girls.
And he said we should follow.
We followed him.
He took us into an old castle, an old German castle.
And he said to us, you pigs!
Here you are going to make a kitchen for us.
And you are going to cook us some food!
Well, I tell you something, if the skies opened and God came down straight, I don't think it would have been a bigger miracle.
We will make a kitchen and cook, and we'll see food?
And he brought us all kinds of food that we should cook for them.
Why, I don't have to tell you that first, they helped themselves, [LAUGHS] of course.
At least we had what to eat.
And when they didn't see it, we could.
And we knew how to cook.
There were a couple of Hungarian girls.
And this lasted for about a couple of weeks that we were cooking for the SS.
And then, of course, we were smuggling back for the girls something also.
